planting.note: Direct sow in loose, stone-free soil once the ground can be worked; make succession sowings.

directions: Sow seed shallowly, about a quarter inch deep, directly in deeply worked, loose soil so the long imperator-type roots can grow straight. Keep the seedbed consistently moist during the slow germination period and thin plants to a couple inches apart once seedlings are established. Harvest these long, slender, nearly coreless roots at around 8 to 9 inches for the sweetest flavor; the deep orange-red color holds up well to steaming, baking, and freezing.

planting.note: Start indoors in late winter and transplant out as soon as the soil is workable; a short-day type best for southern gardens.

directions: Start seed indoors in late winter and transplant seedlings out once the ground can be worked, spacing them four to five inches apart for large bulbs. This is a short-day variety, so grow it in southern regions where it forms bulbs as days lengthen only slightly; the sweet, mild bulbs can reach up to a pound. Keep the soil evenly moist and weed-free, then cure the harvested bulbs in a dry, airy spot before storing.

planting.note: Start indoors with bottom heat; transplant only after nights stay reliably warm.

directions: Start seed indoors with warm bottom heat several weeks before the last frost, since these peppers need heat to germinate well. Transplant the compact, mounding 8-inch plants outdoors only after all danger of frost has passed and nights are warm. Each plant carries dozens of upright, slender 1.5 to 3 inch fruits that ripen from green to red at the same time for a very ornamental, very spicy display; they do well in containers.

planting.note: Direct sow in warm soil after frost, or start indoors a couple weeks early.

directions: Sow seed directly in warm soil after the danger of frost has passed, or start indoors a couple weeks early and transplant carefully. This compact vine saves garden space while producing uniform 1 to 1.25 pound fruits with creamy-white rinds boldly striped in green and orange. Let fruits fully mature and cure the hard rinds before storing; the smooth, sweet flesh is as good for eating as the striking rinds are for autumn displays.

planting.note: Direct sow in spring for summer and fall harvest; heat tolerant.

directions: Direct sow seed in spring for summer and fall harvests, thinning to about an inch apart. These Japanese scallions are long, slender, and mild, forming no true bulb; blanch the stems by hilling soil around the bases for 16 to 18 inch lengths of tender white shank. The heat-tolerant plants grow well in the garden or a container and are excellent sliced into salads, soups, and stir-fries.

planting.note: Direct sow as soon as soil is workable; ideal for shallow beds and containers.

directions: Sow seed shallowly in loose soil as soon as the ground can be worked, keeping the bed evenly moist through the slow germination. This Italian heirloom, whose name means Round of Paris, forms deep-orange, golf-ball-shaped roots that are sweet and tender. Its short, round shape means shallow roots, so it thrives in shallow beds and containers where longer carrots would struggle; harvest young for the best texture.

planting.note: Direct sow or transplant in spring or late summer; flavor sweetens after frost.

directions: Sow seed directly or start transplants for spring and late-summer crops, spacing plants 12 to 18 inches apart. This vigorous hybrid produces tall, upright plants with slightly savoyed leaves and regrows quickly for repeated cut-and-come-again harvests. Collards are cold hardy and grow well far beyond the South; a light frost actually sweetens the nutritious, vitamin-rich leaves, so leave plants standing into cold weather.

planting.note: Direct sow as soon as soil is workable; sow successions for a steady supply.

directions: Direct sow the corky seed clusters as soon as the soil can be worked, thinning seedlings to three or four inches apart since each cluster yields several plants. This golden beet holds its bright color when cooked and is notably sweet and mild, with less of the earthy flavor of red types. Harvest roots when they reach two to three inches across, and enjoy the nutritious tops as cooked greens.

planting.note: Direct sow in spring and again in late summer; more bolt tolerant than most.

directions: Direct sow seed in cool weather for spring and fall crops, thinning to about four to six inches apart. This miniature variety grows just 5 inches tall and is ready to harvest in as few as 30 days, with thick white stems and glossy dark green leaves. It tolerates warm weather better than most bok choy, but grow it quickly and keep it evenly watered to discourage bolting; its compact size suits containers well.

planting.note: Direct sow in warm soil after frost; harvest fruit young and often.

directions: Sow seed directly in warm soil after all danger of frost, spacing plants for good airflow. This zucchini has an open growing habit on compact bush-type plants with reduced spines, making harvest much easier on the hands. It offers intermediate resistance to zucchini yellow mosaic virus and watermelon mosaic virus; pick the tender fruits young and frequently to keep plants producing.

planting.note: Start indoors; transplant out after frost and provide sturdy support.

directions: Start seed indoors about six weeks before your last frost and transplant seedlings out once nights stay warm. This indeterminate cherry vine keeps growing all season, so give it a tall stake or trellis and prune as needed. Each ripe 1.5 inch fruit is nearly translucent and golden with orange-to-pink stripes, bright, fruity, and very sweet with a long shelf life; harvest continuously to encourage new fruit set.

planting.note: Direct sow or transplant in cool weather; sow successions for a steady supply.

directions: Sow seed shallowly in cool weather, since lettuce needs light and cool soil to germinate; make successive sowings for a continuous harvest. This deep-red mini-romaine is compact and easy to grow in small spaces, with uniform, upright leaves that stay clean and are perfect for Caesar salads. It carries disease resistance to downy mildew and lettuce dieback, and part shade helps it hold quality in warmer weather.

planting.note: Direct sow in warm soil after frost; plant in blocks for good pollination.

directions: Sow seed directly in warm soil after frost, planting in blocks of at least four short rows rather than one long row to ensure good wind pollination. This old-fashioned sugary (su) corn produces large 9 inch ears that are very sweet and tender. The plants resist stalk-lodging and are less likely to bend or kink in windy areas; keep them well watered and harvest at the milk stage for peak sweetness.

planting.note: Direct sow in spring and again in late summer for fall harvest.

directions: Direct sow seed in cool weather for spring and fall crops, thinning seedlings to two to four inches apart. This premium Japanese salad turnip forms pure white, smooth-textured roots that are mild and sweet, delicious raw in salads and crudite trays as well as pickled or cooked. Grow it quickly with steady moisture and harvest young as a baby turnip for the best crisp, tender quality.

planting.note: Start indoors and transplant for spring or fall; a self-blanching type.

directions: Start seed indoors and transplant seedlings for a spring or fall crop, keeping plants evenly watered and never letting them check from stress. This self-blanching variety has well-wrapping leaves that curl over the developing curd, shielding the snowy-white heads from sun scald and rain discoloration. It reliably produces uniform, dense, medium-large heads and tolerates heat, so in mild climates it can even be grown nearly year-round.

planting.note: Sow densely in trays indoors year-round; harvest at the first true-leaf stage.

directions: Sow the seed densely across a shallow tray of moist growing mix and keep it evenly moist under bright light. This Asian blend of savory and spicy brassica-type greens is harvested very young, when the first true leaves appear, roughly one to three weeks after sowing. Snip the tender shoots at soil level with scissors and use the complex, umami-rich mix as a garnish for Asian dishes; sow in succession for a steady supply indoors year-round.

planting.note: Sow in trays indoors, or direct sow outdoors in cool weather as a leafy green.

directions: Sow seed densely in a tray for microgreens or thinly outdoors in cool soil to grow as a peppery leafy green. Also called creasy greens, upland cress is high in vitamins A and C and delivers a sharp, watercress-like bite without needing running water. Keep it consistently moist, since dryness makes it more pungent and prone to bolting; harvest microgreens at the true-leaf stage or pick mature outer leaves for sandwiches and salads.

planting.note: Start indoors early; celery needs a long, mild, moist growing season.

directions: Start the tiny seed indoors 10 to 12 weeks before transplanting, pressing it into the surface since it needs light to germinate and is slow to sprout. Celery grows best in climates with long, mild-temperature seasons and demands consistently rich, moist soil to develop tender stalks. Harvest individual stalks when they reach about 6 inches, or wait for the full bunch to mature; keep the plants well watered throughout to avoid stringy, bitter stems.

planting.note: Direct sow in warm soil after frost; give vines room to run.

directions: Sow seed directly in warm soil after all danger of frost, giving the vines plenty of room to spread. This heirloom is unique among winter squashes: when cooked, the flesh separates into spaghetti-like strands that stay tender yet al dente. Let fruits fully ripen to a hard, golden rind and cure them before storing; the mild, low-calorie flesh is delicious with a bit of butter or a favorite sauce.

planting.note: Direct sow or transplant in cool weather; bolt resistant for later sowing.

directions: Sow seed shallowly in cool weather, keeping the soil moist and cool for good germination, and make succession sowings for continuous salads. This emerald-green romaine forms tight heads that reach a striking 12 to 16 inches tall and 4 to 6 inches across, giving large, sturdy leaves ideal for salads and lettuce wraps. Its bolt resistance and vigorous growth let it hold longer into warm weather than many romaines.

planting.note: Start indoors or overwinter in mild areas; a long-day type that matures early.

directions: Start seed indoors in late winter and transplant out once the soil is workable, or sow in fall to overwinter in mild-winter regions. This famous long-day onion traveled from Italy to Walla Walla, Washington, and produces enormous, juicy, sweet bulbs that are one of the best for eating fresh. It matures early enough to grow as an intermediate-day type; these sweet onions do not store long, so enjoy them soon after harvest.

planting.note: Start indoors for spring, or sow in summer for a fall crop; adapted to fall.

directions: Start seed indoors for a spring crop or sow in mid to late summer for a fall harvest, spacing transplants 18 to 24 inches apart. This heirloom is specially adapted to fall growing and produces 4 to 6 inch blue-green central heads followed by a prolific flush of side shoots. Cut the central head while the buds are still tight, then keep harvesting the smaller side shoots for weeks; the plants tolerate frost well.

planting.note: Direct sow in warm soil after frost; give vines plenty of space to run.

directions: Sow seed directly in warm soil after all danger of frost, allowing the long vines room to spread. This classic butternut yields four to five tan, richly sweet fruits per plant, and its solid stems help resist squash vine borers. Let the squash mature fully to a hard rind and cure it in a warm, dry spot; properly cured butternut stores for months and is excellent roasted, baked, or pureed into soup.

planting.note: Direct sow as soon as the soil can be worked; more heat tolerant than most peas.

directions: Direct sow seed as soon as the ground can be worked in spring, since peas germinate and grow best in cool soil. Introduced in 1943 as a cross between Laxton's Progress and Perfection, this heirloom is both cold tolerant for early sowing and more heat tolerant than most peas, extending the season on both ends. The sturdy 24 to 30 inch plants carry seven to nine medium peas per pod; provide light support and pick regularly to keep them producing.

planting.note: Direct sow in spring; cold hardy and can overwinter in many areas.

directions: Direct sow seed in spring for summer harvests, thinning to about an inch apart, or leave clumps to overwinter. This 2016 All-America Selections winner is a cold-hardy bunching onion with crisp texture and a strong, pungent flavor, and it resists pink root rot. Harvest by pulling whole clusters of upright green stalks, or cut individual stems and let the plants regrow for repeated cut-and-come-again picking.

planting.note: Soak seed, then sow densely in trays indoors year-round for juicing.

directions: Soak the hard red winter wheat seed overnight, then spread it densely over a tray of moist growing mix and keep it evenly moist and bright. The seed sprouts rapidly into lush green blades that are ready to cut in about eight to twelve days, when they reach 6 to 9 inches tall. Snip the grass at the base for juicing, which yields a drink rich in vitamins, minerals, and chlorophyll; sow fresh trays in succession for a continuous supply.

planting.note: Direct sow in succession spring through summer; heat and cold tolerant.

directions: Direct sow seed in succession from spring through summer, thinning to about an inch apart for a steady supply of scallions. Popular since the 1700s, this dependable heirloom green onion thrives in a wide range of soils and tolerates both heat and cold. Blanch the stems by hilling soil around the bases for longer white shanks, and harvest young for mild flavor before any bulb forms.

planting.note: Direct sow in spring or late summer; flavor sweetens with cold weather.

directions: Direct sow or transplant this kale in spring or late summer, spacing plants 12 to 18 inches apart. Introduced in 1994, White Russian is a white-ribbed cross between Red Russian and Siberian kale that grows especially sweet in cool weather and stands up well to frost. Harvest young leaves for tender baby greens or let plants mature for full-sized leaves; a hard frost only improves the sweet flavor.

planting.note: Direct sow very early in spring, or in fall in mild-winter regions.

directions: Direct sow seed very early in spring as soon as the soil can be worked, or in fall where winters are mild, since fava beans need cool weather to set pods. The sturdy, upright plants bear ornamental white-and-black flowers and reach several feet tall. Eat the young pods like snap beans, pinch the tender growing tips as a spring green, or shell the beans and cook them while still green for a sweet, buttery treat.

planting.note: Start indoors in winter; a short-day type for southern gardens.

directions: Start seed indoors in winter and transplant to the garden while still cool, since this is a short-day onion that bulbs best in southern regions. For really sweet, mild bulbs, grow it in soil that is low in sulfur; this pink-root-resistant type is among the few approved to be sold as a Vidalia onion. Keep plants weed-free and evenly watered, and cure the harvested bulbs before use, as sweet onions do not store long.

common_name: Yellow Sweet Spanish Utah Bulb Onion

planting.note: Start indoors and transplant out; a long-day type for northern gardens.

directions: Start seed indoors in late winter and transplant out as soon as the soil can be worked, spacing plants four to six inches apart. This long-day heirloom, grown in the U.S. since at least 1916, forms large 3 to 6 inch globe-shaped bulbs with mild, sweet flavor and grows best in states north of the 37th parallel. Keep the soil evenly moist during bulbing, then cure the bulbs in a dry, airy place before storing.

planting.note: Start indoors from seed in late winter and transplant out in early spring.

directions: Start seed indoors in late winter and transplant seedlings out in early spring, spacing them several inches apart. This eschalion, or banana-type shallot, produces large, elongated, easy-to-peel bulbs with coppery-pink skin and a sweet, mild flavor prized by chefs. Grow it much like an onion with steady moisture and weed control, then cure the harvested bulbs in a dry, airy spot; they store well for months.

planting.note: Start indoors early; slow to germinate and frost tender when young.

directions: Start the fine seed indoors well before the last frost, surface-sowing it into a well-drained mix and keeping it warm, as germination is slow and uneven. Grow this florist's silver dollar eucalyptus in full sun with lean, well-drained soil once transplanted; it is frost tender and grown as an annual in cold regions. Harvest the long stems of rounded, dusty blue-green leaves for fresh or dried arrangements, where they hold up well and carry a menthol-like fragrance.

planting.note: Direct sow where it is to grow, since dill resents transplanting.

directions: Direct sow seed where the plants are to grow, since dill has a taproot and resents being transplanted; make succession sowings for a steady supply. This variety is especially good for pickling thanks to its early, abundant seed heads, while the aromatic foliage is delicious on potatoes, fish, and vegetables. Let some plants flower to attract pollinators and support swallowtail butterfly caterpillars, for which dill is an important host plant.

planting.note: Start indoors and transplant out; a hardy perennial in most zones.

directions: Start seed indoors and transplant seedlings into full sun and well-drained soil, spacing them 18 to 24 inches apart. This drought-tolerant, hardy perennial has bold, assertive, savory flavor valued for seasoning rich foods like pork, poultry, and cheese, plus stuffing, soups, and stews. It also bears edible purple-blue flowers that draw pollinators; cut plants back in spring to keep them bushy and productive year after year.

planting.note: Start indoors with warmth; transplant out after frost as a tender plant.

directions: Start seed indoors with warmth and transplant the bushy plants out only after the danger of frost has passed. Stevia, also called sweetleaf, produces leaves up to 300 times sweeter than sugar, giving a calorie-free, low-glycemic sweetener with a faint anise aftertaste. Grow it in full sun and pinch the tips to encourage branching; harvest leaves before flowering for peak sweetness and dry them for storage, or overwinter potted plants indoors in cold climates.

planting.note: Start indoors and transplant out after frost, or direct sow in warm soil.

directions: Start seed indoors and transplant out after all danger of frost, or direct sow once the soil is warm, spacing plants about a foot apart. This dual-purpose basil combines robust culinary flavor with large, richly colored maroon flower heads that make it a striking accent in mixed containers and bouquets. Pinch the plants often to keep them bushy; its strong aroma means a little goes a long way in cooking, and it can be grown indoors on a sunny sill.

planting.note: Start indoors or direct sow; a hardy perennial that returns each year.

directions: Sow seed indoors or direct sow in clusters, since chives grow best planted in small clumps rather than single plants. This easy, low-maintenance perennial tolerates drought and returns year after year, offering hollow foliage with a mild onion flavor for salads, potatoes, and eggs. The edible lavender flowers attract bees, make a flavorful chive vinegar, and dry well for arrangements; snip the leaves regularly to keep fresh growth coming.

planting.note: Start indoors and transplant; a vigorous perennial best contained in pots.

directions: Start seed indoors and transplant seedlings out, ideally into a buried pot or contained bed, since mint spreads aggressively by runners. Grow it in moist soil and partial to full sun; the aromatic leaves enhance iced tea, mixed drinks, and savory dishes like meats, stews, and vegetable fritters. Cut plants back regularly to encourage tender new growth, and let some tiny flowers bloom to feed the bees.

planting.note: Start indoors; surface sow since seed needs light, and transplant out.

directions: Start the tiny seed indoors, pressing it onto the surface since it needs light to germinate, then transplant into full sun and well-drained soil. This common oregano, also called wild marjoram, has a milder flavor than the pungent Greek type and is the traditional variety for medicinal use. It is a hardy perennial with pretty pink to lavender flowers that draw pollinators; harvest leaves before flowering for the strongest flavor, fresh or dried.

planting.note: Direct sow where it is to grow, since fennel resents transplanting.

directions: Direct sow seed where it is to grow, since fennel forms a taproot and dislikes being transplanted; keep the soil evenly moist. This bulbing fennel is easy to grow and slow to bolt, producing big, sweet, succulent bulbs that are excellent raw, added to soups and pasta, or charred on the grill. Hill soil around the swelling bases to keep them tender and white, and let the feathery fronds attract pollinators.
